### Post by evets on 2008-01-27
It was working in Feisty, but after upgrading to Gutsy it quit. I upgraded, after that, to Hardy (now at Alpha 3), and it's still inop.

It's a Toshiba R-15 S829 Tablet PC. It's worked since Dapper for over a year now, never had an issue with it until now.

Any advice?

### Post by rg_stephens on 2008-01-28
I had the same issue with my HP Tablet after upgrading to 7.10. I got it working just by uncommenting the following two lines:

# InputDevice "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
# InputDevice "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"

---

### Post by evets on 2008-01-28
Thanks, but when I do that and log out then in, like I posted, I just keep getting sent back to the login, over and over.

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-28
Do you have the device /dev/input/wacom?  If so,  is it actually controlled by your pen?   Try
```
sudo cat /dev/input/wacom
```
After typing this in, try to use the pen.
If you don't have the wacom device, try the above  command on all the "event"s  in /dev/input  until you get a response,  then replace the device in xorg.conf  (i.e. replace /dev/input/wacom with /dev/input/event0) and restart x.

---

As far as trying with the "events", can you explain that a little further for me? I can't even open any of the "events" files, not even using sudo gedit.

I'm probably not doing it correctly.

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-28
Sorry, I should have been more clear - I was actually writing that post just before class started, so I was rushing.

After typing in "sudo cat /dev/input/wacom", try moving the pen across the screen.  If it responds, you should end up with a bunch of garbage characters in the terminal (just ctrl+c to get out of it, and if it turns everything in the terminal to gibberish, just close and reopen the terminal).  If it doesn't respond, then that means that /dev/input/wacom isn't linked to your pen, so having that device in xorg.conf won't do anything.

To figure out which device is controlling the pen, first get a list of all the input devices (type in ls /dev/input - on my computer, I get:
```
:~$ls /dev/input/
by-id    event0  event2  event4  event6  mouse0  mouse2         wacom
by-path  event1  event3  event5  mice    mouse1 
```
Then try typing "sudo cat /dev/input/event0", run the pen over the screen, and see if you get anything (non-response would be as you describe, a blinking cursor).  Go through each of the devices until you find one that responds.  Be careful you don't touch any other input devices (keyboard or trackpad) at the same time.  In theory, you should be able to replace all the references in xorg.conf to /dev/input/wacom with /dev/input/event# (whichever one works), and it should work.  Don't forget to uncomment the lines with "SendCoreEvents" in them.

Hopefully this works, if not, then there's something else going on that's a problem.  What happens if you type in "sudo wacdump /dev/input/wacom"?  It's strange that you have that device, yet you get booted back to the login screen when you try to use it.

---

I think you're right, I have something else going on. Just a guess, since I'm still uneducated with Linux, but I think it's related to my xorg issues.

Briefly...

I have a Tablet PC with it's own screen. I also have a Samsung 205BW wide screen. Ny video chipset is Intel 82852/855GM (Integrated Graphics). 

In order to get the proper resolution (1680X1050) and refresh rate, I have to use the Ubuntu Experimenting Modesetting Driver  for Intel Chipsets. But, using these, I have two issues...1) High CPU usage for xorg and slow (jerky) movement of screens and graphics, webpages, etc) and 2) My 2nd screen "radio Buttin" on the Screens and Graphics GUI is greyed out, and I can't use the Tablet screen. 

OTOH, if I use the Intel i810 Driver, I can "tick" the 2nd screen button, and use both screens. I was using the i810 driver, and found a resolution that was ok, but still, I had no pen. So I tried to uncomment the appropriate sections in xorg and since then, I can't use the i810 drivers at all. In fact, as I posted above, I will be sent back to the login screen over and over, forcing me to use the kernel recovery mode and x-setup option to get back in.  Which makes me use a "default" setting, and wash, rinse, repeat...LOL

To make sure I'm doing this correctly...

First I open a terminal, and type sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Then I remove the # in front of the lines in the section pertaining to the wacom.

Then I save it as xorg.conf

The I log out, and (attempt) to log in.

Does that sound right?

Again, I really do appreciate you taking your time to help me.

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-28
I spent forever trying to get my various tablet devices to work, so I hope I can help.

However, that is a really weird output from wacdump.  You should get something like this (although this is from a desktop with an external tablet):
```
wacdump v0.7.4                                                                  

MODEL=Wacom Graphire2 4x5               ROM=2.0-3
CLS=USB  VNDR=Wacom  DEV=Graphire2  SUB=ET_0405




TOOLTYPE=NONE                            IN_PROX=out
  BUTTON=+00000 (+00000 .. +00000)         POS_X=+00000 (+00000 .. +10206)
   POS_Y=+00000 (+00000 .. +07422)      DISTANCE=+00000 (+00000 .. +00063)
PRESSURE=+00000 (+00000 .. +00511)      RELWHEEL=+00000 (-00001 .. +00001)

    LEFT=             MIDDLE=              RIGHT=              EXTRA=
    SIDE=              TOUCH=             STYLUS=            STYLUS2=
     BT0=                BT1=                BT2=                BT3=
     BT4=                BT5=                BT6=                BT7=
     BT8=                BT9=               BT10=               BT11=
    BT12=               BT13=               BT14=               BT15=
    BT16=               BT17=               BT18=               BT19=
    BT20=               BT21=               BT22=               BT23=   
```

Perhaps something is messed up with wacom-tools?  Try running "sudo apt-get --reinstall install wacom-tools" - I'm not sure if it'll do anything, but it can't hurt.  Beyond that, we're probably going to have to look at the error messages that you get when you get kicked back to the login screen (located at /var/log/xorg.0.log).

By the way, you're not uncommenting the line "# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet", are you?  If so, that would cause Xorg to freak out.  You just want to uncomment the three lines below it.

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-28
I'm afraid I can't really be of much more help - I've never had issues like that with wacdump.  However, google came up with [this]("http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-520413-highlight-.html?sid=3b9bebebe1d06ce248f06199f1b3eeb7") discussion, and someone seems to have a fix in the last post.

I just realized that since you're using a tablet pc, it won't be one of the devices in /dev/input.  You can find out which device /dev/input/wacom is pointing to by typing in "ls -l /dev/input/wacom" - it should point to /dev/ttys* or something similar.  Maybe try that device instead of the ttys3 in the forum post that I linked?

---

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-28
Well... that's something, anyway...

After your groundhog day adventure, can you log in to failsafe gnome (in "options->change session" on the login screen) and then post the results of "cat /var/log/Xorg.0.log | grep EE"?  That should tell us what X doesn't like about your wacom configuration.

Edit: I realized you might not be able to get into failsafe gnome.  In that case, try just going to tty1 (ctrl+alt+F1) and logging in and running the command from there.  You can get back to your login screen with ctrl+alt+F7.

---

### Post by Curtisc on 2008-01-29
No errors in Xorg?  Very strange indeed.  Do you have any custom configuration files for the tablet?  If so, you might try renaming them (i.e. mv .xinitrc .xinitrc.old, or whatever the script is called) so that they don't get loaded.  It's a long shot, but maybe try the same thing with the gnome custom config folders (mkdir oldconf && mv .gnome .gnome2 .gconf .gconfd .metacity oldconf).

---
